checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains text that is present in the source document (the header list and the footer table). No fabricated sentences or paragraphs were identified.
The source document contains no contact information (phone, email, URLs). The output JSON correctly reflects this by not including any such information.

The document consists of a single page containing a text list, a diagram, and a footer table. All these elements are represented in the JSON (either as text elements or image elements). No entire page or section is missing.
The footer table in the source has two rows of information. The output JSON table correctly captures both rows with the appropriate cell contents.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The network diagram in the center of the page contains a substantial amount of technical text and labels (e.g., VLAN identifiers, server names like 'NCID', 'XiQ', and functional labels like 'OOB Management', 'Data Center'). None of this text is captured in the JSON as text elements. The image alt text for the main diagram is too generic and fails to convey this specific information, resulting in a loss of more than 25% of the document's total content.

checks.b_evidence_output: [The listed strings are entirely missing from the extracted text elements in the JSON]

checks.a_analysis: I have carefully reviewed the source PDF and the JSON output. The text from the SSID list and the bottom table appears only once in the JSON, matching the source. While the diagram is fragmented into many small images (reading order 4-52), the text contained within them is not repeated as separate text blocks in the JSON. No text duplication was found.

The converted document lacks any heading elements. According to the structural requirements, H1 should be used for the document title to facilitate screen reader navigation. Although a title exists in the metadata and a clear descriptive label ('Description:WLAN Service – Current') exists in the content, no H1 or other heading levels were created, resulting in a flat and inaccessible structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: The document contains a descriptive title area in the bottom table: 'Description:WLAN Service – Current'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'elements' array contains no objects with "type": "heading". All sections have "title": null.

checks.a_analysis: The table at the bottom of the document is structurally corrupted. In the source, the table has a 3-column grid in the first row. The second row has two cells, the second of which spans the width of the last two columns of the first row. The JSON output defines three cells in the first row and two in the second, but assigns all cells a 'colspan' of 1. This creates an invalid and inconsistent table grid (3 columns in row 1 vs. 2 columns in row 2).
checks.b_evidence_source: The footer table has 3 columns in the top row ('Date', 'Project', 'Created By') and 2 columns in the bottom row ('Department', 'Description').
checks.b_evidence_output: The table at element index 1 has a first row with 3 cells and a second row with 2 cells, all with "colspan": 1.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order in the JSON diverges significantly from the logical flow of the source document. The metadata table, which is located at the very bottom of the source PDF as a footer, has been moved to the second position in the reading order (reading_order: 2), appearing before the main diagram content (reading_order: 3). This disrupts the intended logical flow of information.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table containing 'Date', 'Project', and 'Created By' is located at the bottom of the page.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table is assigned "reading_order": 2, while the main diagram is assigned "reading_order": 3.

checks.a_analysis: The list of SSID descriptions at the top of the page is correctly converted into an unordered list. Although other text blocks in the source (like 'VLANs & Subnets') resemble lists and are missing from the JSON, the list that was captured is structurally sound.
No links are present in the source document, and none have been incorrectly injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF contains a complex network topology diagram where the spatial organization of elements (Users, Access Points, Networks, Data Center) communicates the flow of data and connectivity. In the HTML output, this organization is completely destroyed. The diagram is decomposed into a long, single-column vertical list of disconnected image fragments and text blocks. A user cannot understand the relationship between the components or follow the intended visual logic of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The unified network topology diagram showing connectivity between 'Guests'/'State Employees' on the left and 'Internet Access'/'Agency Networks'/'State Network' on the right.
checks.b_evidence_output: A vertical series of fragmented images (green blurs, red blurs) and text blocks that strip away all spatial relationships and directional arrows present in the original diagram.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF features a metadata table at the bottom with fields for Date, Project, Department, etc. The HTML output renders this information at the top of the page in a clear, labeled format. While the styling is simplified, the data remains organized and readable to a sighted user.

The primary meaningful image in the document is the network diagram. The conversion process has 'broken' this image by slicing it into numerous small, unrecognizable fragments (e.g., blurs of color without context). These fragments do not represent the original content and fail to communicate the information contained in the source diagram. While the State Seal is rendered correctly as a single image, the failure to render the central diagram as a coherent visual unit constitutes a major image rendering defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The central 'WLAN Service – Current' network topology diagram.
checks.b_evidence_output: Multiple separate elements showing only pieces of the diagram, such as a large green blur (from the 'Agency Networks' cloud) and a red blur (from the 'Internet Access' cloud), separated by white space and other fragments.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the HTML output and the source PDF; no instances of duplicated body content (paragraphs or the same diagram fragments repeated) were observed.
